summary: Individuals with ADHD frequently show excessive activation and reduced task-related suppression in Default Mode Network (DMN) hubs, termed 'DMN interference,' which is linked to attentional lapses.
claims: DMN often fails to deactivate properly during attention-demanding tasks.
Precuneus and mPFC frequently exhibit persistent hyperconnectivity.
Aberrant DMN–FPN anticorrelation is a robust predictor of inattentive symptoms.

summary: Weakened long-range connectivity between the Frontoparietal Control Network (FPN) and Dorsal Attention Network (DAN) is often observed, contributing to deficits in top-down attentional control.
claims: Reduced intra-FPN connectivity and diminished coupling with DAN are common findings.
Impaired top-down control affects the ability to shift attention voluntarily.
Hypoconnectivity in these networks correlates with the severity of clinical inattention.

title: Motor and cerebellar network dysfunction
summary: Abnormal connectivity in somatomotor and cerebellar networks reflects deficits in motor control and temporal processing (timing), which are frequent endophenotypes of ADHD.
claims: Reduced Precentral–Cerebellar coupling impairs sensorimotor integration.
Hyperconnectivity within SMN is often linked to hyperactivity/impulsivity.
Cerebellar hypoactivation is associated with deficits in interval timing tasks.

summary: Dysfunction in the Salience Network (AI–ACC) can disrupt the 'switching' mechanism between DMN and FPN, contributing to impaired response inhibition.
claims: Reduced connectivity between Anterior Insula (AI) and ACC decreases salience detection.
Diminished SN–FPN coupling impairs engagement with task-relevant stimuli.
Abnormal insula activation correlates with impulsivity scores in many cohorts.

summary: Longitudinal studies suggest a pattern of 'delayed maturation' in ADHD, particularly in frontostriatal circuits and the segregation of DMN–FPN systems.
claims: Frontostriatal connectivity tends to increase more slowly with age compared to controls.
DMN–FPN anticorrelation often reaches mature levels later than in typical development.
Delayed network segregation provides a neural basis for persistent symptoms into adolescence.

summary: Individuals with ADHD who have higher IQs often exhibit partial normalization or compensatory hyperconnectivity in prefrontal and parietal regions, supporting better cognitive control.
claims: Enhanced DLPFC–Parietal coupling is observed in high-IQ ADHD groups.
Compensatory activation may mitigate observed attentional lapses.
Cognitive reserve may mask underlying connectivity deficits in behavioral performance.
